title: Quiz: Strategy Backtesting
description: Test your understanding of backtesting methodology

questions.question: What is survivorship bias in backtesting?

options.text: Only testing on assets that survived to the present day
Using too short a time period

questions.explanation: Survivorship bias occurs when backtests only include assets that survived (weren't delisted or went bankrupt). This overstates returns because failed companies are excluded from the analysis.

questions.question: What is look-ahead bias?

options.text: Predicting too far into the future
Using information that wasn't available at the time of decision

questions.explanation: Look-ahead bias occurs when a backtest uses information that wouldn't have been available at the time, such as using revised economic data instead of initial estimates, or future prices in calculations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of walk-forward analysis?

options.text: To validate strategy robustness using rolling train/test periods

questions.explanation: Walk-forward analysis trains a strategy on one period and tests on the next, rolling forward through time. This validates that the strategy works on truly out-of-sample data and isn't overfit.

questions.question: What indicates potential overfitting?

options.text: Consistent performance across train and test periods
Large performance degradation from training to testing

questions.explanation: When a strategy performs much better in training than in testing, it suggests overfitting - the strategy learned patterns specific to the training data that don't generalize to new data.

questions.question: Backtests should ignore slippage for more accurate results.
questions.explanation: False. Slippage is a real cost in trading and should always be modeled in backtests. Ignoring slippage leads to overly optimistic performance estimates, especially for high-frequency strategies.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of Monte Carlo validation?

options.text: It tests if results could be achieved by random chance
It eliminates the need for out-of-sample testing

questions.explanation: Monte Carlo validation shuffles returns to create many random scenarios, then checks if the strategy's results are statistically significant or could have been achieved by luck.

questions.question: How should transaction costs be modeled for realistic backtests?

options.text: Include commission, slippage, and market impact
Use fixed dollar amounts regardless of trade size

questions.explanation: Realistic backtests should model commission (brokerage fees), slippage (difference between expected and actual price), and market impact (price movement caused by your own trading).

questions.question: Which practices help avoid overfitting? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Limiting the number of strategy parameters
Adding more parameters until results improve

questions.explanation: To avoid overfitting: test on out-of-sample data, use fewer parameters, and validate across multiple time periods. Adding parameters to improve results is actually a cause of overfitting.
